CLARITY.justification: The paper is well organized (intro → method → experiments → conclusion) and clearly states its main contributions early (introduction of Batch Policy Gradient and key algorithmic components such as ρ=πθ/q, λ-returns, and value estimators). Notation appears consistent and well-defined in the methodology (importance weights, returns, and value-function estimators are explicitly described). Results, baselines, and evaluation metrics are presented clearly (synthetic and real datasets, AMT scores, statistical tests), and the writing quality is reported as good though not described as exceptional, suggesting only minor presentation issues remain.

ORIGINALITY.justification: The paper combines existing ingredients (importance sampling for off-policy correction, λ-returns, and GTD-style value estimation) into a tailored Batch Policy Gradient (BPG) algorithm for Seq2Seq conversation models in an offline, limited-reward setting. While adapting these techniques to the practical constraints of noisy, scarce labels and batch/off-line training for chatbots is useful, the core methods are established in RL and prior dialogue work, so the contribution reads as an incremental, domain-specific integration rather than a fundamentally new algorithmic idea. The authors do clearly differentiate their setup and evaluate component choices (ρ, λ, V̂), which would be informative but not surprising to an expert.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: The main claims are clearly stated and supported by experiments on a synthetic Europarl task and a real restaurant recommendation dataset, with comparisons to reasonable baselines (ML, BPG-NIS, OPG) and appropriate statistical tests reported. The proposed use of importance sampling, λ-returns, and GTD-style value estimation is standard for off-policy batch policy-gradients and—based on the summary—appears correctly applied; there is no indication of incorrect mathematical derivations. Minor technical concerns remain (high variance from importance weights, clipping at 5 and very small step sizes introducing bias, and simple/limited value-function estimates), but these issues are acknowledged in the paper and do not appear to invalidate the experimental conclusions.

main_claims: Introduce Batch Policy Gradient (BPG), an off-policy batch policy-gradient algorithm for improving Seq2Seq RNN chatbots using limited, noisy labelled rewards.
Use importance sampling (ρ), λ-returns, and value-function estimators (constant or GTD(λ)) to perform off-policy corrections and reduce variance in batch updates.

Demonstrate that BPG outperforms on-policy or non-importance-sampled variants and maximum-likelihood baselines on a synthetic Europarl task and on a real-world restaurant recommendation (customer service) dataset.
Show that unlabelled data can be used to initialize policies via maximum likelihood and that small amounts of labelled data (scored by humans via AMT) can be used to refine these policies with BPG.
_summary.methodology: Propose BPG: compute gradients of a batch objective J(θ)=∑_s q(s)V^{πθ}(s) and approximate ∇J via importance-weighted policy gradients using ρ=πθ/q, λ-returns for future rewards, and a value-function estimate V̂ (constant or GTD(λ)); perform mini-batch updates on top LSTM/softmax parameters of Seq2Seq models.
datasets: Online restaurant recommendation customer-service conversation dataset (restaurant recommendations)
Amazon Mechanical Turk (AMT) labelled responses (used as rewards)
baselines: On-line Policy Gradient (OPG) (Degris et al. style on-line off-policy implementation)
metrics: Mean AMT score on 0/1/2 scale (real dataset)
limitations: Rewards are noisy and expensive to obtain, so labelled data is limited (explicitly stated).
Batch/off-line setting: cannot interact with the environment—future actions/rewards must come from data (explicitly stated).
Importance sampling increases variance of updates, requiring clipping (they clip weights at 5) and very small step sizes to avoid instability (explicitly stated).

Estimating the value function is difficult due to large state space and limited labels; they resort to simple estimators (constant or GTD(λ)) and note imperfect value estimates may persist (explicitly stated).
_summary.topic_area: NLP - neural conversation models / reinforcement learning for chatbots
